title: Fertilizer Runoff → Eutrophication → Dead Zone

steps.label: Fertilizer applied to crops
Nitrate/phosphate runoff enters waterway
Algal bloom covers surface
Sunlight blocked → aquatic plants die
Decomposers consume dissolved oxygen
Hypoxia — dissolved oxygen drops below 2 mg/L
Fish kill → dead zone
Buffer strips, reduce fertilizer, wetland restoration
Lower crop yield or added cost for farmers

title: Fossil Fuel Combustion → Enhanced Greenhouse Effect → Climate Change

steps.label: Combustion of coal, oil, natural gas
CO₂ released to atmosphere
CO₂ absorbs and re-emits infrared radiation
Enhanced greenhouse effect → global temperature rise
Sea level rise, ice melt, extreme weather, habitat shifts
Positive feedback: permafrost thaw releases CH₄
Renewable energy, carbon tax, efficiency standards, reforestation
Economic disruption, job losses in fossil fuel sector, higher energy costs short-term

title: CFCs → Ozone Depletion → Increased UV Radiation

steps.label: CFCs released from aerosols, refrigerants, foam
CFCs rise to stratosphere
UV breaks CFC → chlorine radical (Cl·) released
Cl· catalyzes O₃ breakdown (one Cl· destroys ~100,000 O₃)
Ozone layer thins → increased UV-B reaches surface
Skin cancer, cataracts, crop damage, phytoplankton decline
Montreal Protocol (1987) — banned CFC production
Replacement HFCs are potent greenhouse gases

title: Mining → Habitat Destruction → Water Pollution

steps.label: Surface or subsurface mining operation
Overburden removed, sulfide minerals exposed
Acid mine drainage: sulfuric acid + heavy metals
Contaminated waterways, aquatic habitat destruction
Soil erosion, tailings storage, habitat fragmentation
Reclamation, water treatment, regulation (SMCRA)
Reclamation is expensive and may not restore original ecosystem

title: Urbanization → Impervious Surfaces → Flooding/Pollution

steps.label: Urban development: roads, parking lots, buildings
Impervious surfaces prevent infiltration
Rapid surface runoff during rainfall
Increased flooding, erosion, combined sewer overflows
Oil, sediment, nutrients carried to waterways
Urban heat island from reduced evapotranspiration
Permeable pavement, green roofs, rain gardens, retention ponds
Higher construction costs, maintenance requirements

title: Deforestation → Carbon Release → Enhanced Greenhouse Effect

steps.label: Trees cleared for agriculture, logging, development
Stored carbon in biomass released as CO₂ (burning/decomposition)
Carbon sink removed — less CO₂ absorbed from atmosphere
Increased atmospheric CO₂ concentration
Enhanced greenhouse effect → global warming
Reforestation, sustainable forestry, REDD+ programs
Reduces agricultural land, economic costs for developing nations

title: Fossil Fuel Combustion → SO₂/NOₓ → Acid Rain

steps.label: Coal-fired power plants and vehicle exhaust
SO₂ and NOₓ released into atmosphere
React with water → sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) and nitric acid (HNO₃)
Acid precipitation (pH < 5.6)
Aquatic acidification → fish and amphibian decline
Soil nutrient leaching (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) → forest decline
Scrubbers, catalytic converters, Clean Air Act
Scrubbers produce waste sludge; catalytic converters require rare metals

title: Vehicle Emissions → Photochemical Smog → Respiratory Illness

steps.label: Vehicle exhaust releases NOₓ and VOCs
UV sunlight drives photochemical reactions
Ground-level ozone (O₃) and PANs formed
Respiratory problems: asthma, bronchitis, reduced lung function
Crop damage, reduced visibility
Catalytic converters, public transit, emission standards
Vehicle emission controls increase vehicle cost

title: Persistent Toxin → Bioaccumulation → Biomagnification

steps.label: DDT, mercury, or PCBs released into environment
Toxin absorbed by organisms; stored in fatty tissues
Bioaccumulation: toxin concentration increases in individual over lifetime
Predator eats many contaminated prey → higher concentration
Biomagnification: top predators have highest toxin levels
DDT → thin eggshells in bald eagles; mercury → neurological damage in humans from tuna
Ban persistent chemicals, reduce point-source pollution, biomonitoring
Replacement chemicals may have unknown long-term effects

title: CO₂ Absorption → Ocean Acidification → Coral/Shell Decline

steps.label: Atmospheric CO₂ increases from fossil fuel combustion
Ocean absorbs ~30% of atmospheric CO₂
CO₂ + H₂O → carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) → lower pH
Reduced carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) availability
Shell-forming organisms (corals, mollusks) cannot build CaCO₃ structures
Coral bleaching, reef collapse, marine food web disruption
Reduce CO₂ emissions, marine protected areas, alkalinity enhancement research
Even with emission reductions, ocean pH recovery takes centuries

title: Species Introduction → Invasive Spread → Ecosystem Disruption

steps.label: Non-native species introduced (intentionally or accidentally)
No natural predators, competitors, or diseases in new environment
Rapid population growth — often generalist species
Outcompetes native species for resources
Native species decline, ecosystem structure altered
Early detection, removal programs, trade regulations, biological control
Biological control agents may themselves become invasive

title: Pesticide Use → Resistance → Pesticide Treadmill

steps.label: Broad-spectrum pesticide applied to crops
Most pest individuals killed; resistant individuals survive
Resistant individuals reproduce → resistant population
Farmer applies more pesticide or stronger chemical
Pesticide treadmill: escalating chemical use, ecosystem contamination
Integrated Pest Management (IPM): biological control, crop rotation, targeted application
IPM is more labor-intensive and requires knowledge/monitoring

title: Power Plant Cooling Water → Thermal Pollution → Aquatic Stress

steps.label: Power plant or industrial facility uses water for cooling
Heated water discharged into river or lake
Water temperature increases locally
Dissolved oxygen decreases (warmer water holds less O₂)
Aquatic organisms stressed; cold-water species (trout) may die
Cooling towers, cooling ponds, discharge regulations
Cooling towers are expensive and use water for evaporation

title: Population Growth → Resource Depletion → Environmental Degradation

steps.label: Human population grows exponentially
Increased demand for food, water, energy, land
Intensified agriculture, deforestation, fossil fuel extraction
Habitat loss, soil degradation, water pollution, climate change
Reduced biodiversity, ecosystem service decline
Family planning, education, sustainable development, efficiency improvements
Development programs require funding; cultural barriers to family planning

title: Disturbance → Ecological Succession → Climax Community

steps.label: Major disturbance (fire, volcano, glacier retreat, abandoned farmland)
Pioneer species colonize (lichens, mosses, grasses)
Soil builds up, conditions change, new species arrive
Species diversity and biomass increase over time
Climax community established (mature forest in many biomes)
Prescribed burns maintain some ecosystems in earlier stages
Succession takes decades to centuries; human disturbance resets it

title: Dam Construction → Altered River Ecosystem

steps.label: Hydroelectric dam built across river
River flow altered; reservoir created upstream
Fish migration blocked (salmon cannot reach spawning grounds)
Sediment trapped behind dam → downstream erosion
Reservoir decomposition releases methane; communities displaced
Fish ladders, run-of-river designs, dam removal in some cases
Fish ladders have limited effectiveness; dam removal is costly and disruptive

title: Overgrazing → Desertification

steps.label: Livestock overgrazing on semi-arid grassland
Vegetation removed faster than it can regrow
Soil exposed to wind and water erosion
Topsoil lost; land becomes barren
Desertification — productive land becomes desert-like
Rotational grazing, reduced stocking rates, revegetation
Reduced herd size impacts ranchers' livelihoods

title: Ocean Warming → Coral Bleaching → Reef Collapse

steps.label: Global temperatures increase from greenhouse gas emissions
Ocean absorbs heat; sea surface temperature rises
Coral expels symbiotic zooxanthellae algae (stress response)
Coral turns white (bleached) and loses food source
Prolonged bleaching → coral death → reef ecosystem collapse
Reduce emissions, marine protected areas, coral restoration, reduce local stressors
Even with protection, warming may outpace coral adaptation

title: Irrigation → Salinization → Crop Yield Decline

steps.label: Flood or furrow irrigation in arid region
Water evaporates, dissolved salts left behind in soil
Salt concentration increases in topsoil
Osmotic stress on plants — roots cannot absorb water
Crop yield decline, eventual land abandonment
Drip irrigation (reduces evaporation), soil flushing, salt-tolerant crops
Drip irrigation has higher upfront cost; soil flushing uses more water

title: Nuclear Fission → Radioactive Waste → Long-term Storage Challenge

steps.label: Uranium fuel undergoes nuclear fission in reactor
Fission produces heat (electricity) + radioactive byproducts
Spent fuel rods remain radioactive for thousands of years
Requires long-term secure storage (deep geologic repository)
Risk of contamination from leaks, accidents, or natural disasters
Dry cask storage, vitrification, proposed deep geologic repositories (Yucca Mountain)
No community wants a nuclear waste repository nearby (NIMBY); political stalemate
